comments.title: Fear
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What advice would you have for someone from a Catholic background who is experiencing a strong fear of hell, of eternal damnation?

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
Start by remembering how that fear was programmed into you. It's not a natural fear; the Roman church has been systematically inflicting it on children for centuries, partly in a misguided belief that terrorizing them will make them less likely to sin, partly in an all too accurate belief that terrorizing them will make it easier for the church bureaucracy to extract obedience and money from them. Take some time to recall how exactly you were taught that fear. Journal about it, but don't try to argue with it -- it's not a rational thing. It's the stark terror of a small child who has undergone systematic emotional abuse by people he or she was told to trust.

As you explore the memories, you'll reach a point at which you'll be furiously angry at what was done to you. Accept that and explore it, too. There may be several layers of fear, anger, shame, and grief all mixed up together. Take your time, be patient with yourself, and let it unravel. On the far side are ways to relate to the Divine through love and trust, renouncing the false notion of God as a sort of ultimate domestic abuser who demands endless cringing flattery so he doesn't beat you up forever.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
Well said.

Not OP... so, I guess the same or similar can be done for those of us who were taught that when you are dead you are dead, and you won't wake up until you are resurrected from the earth at the time of the second coming, when you will face judgment from God/Jesus and can either join with the true believers of the one true faith or face torment in the lake of fire.

I'm mostly over it thanks to my own journaling and involvement in alternative spirtuality, extensive journaling since I was a kid, and the Octagon Society... but you know, there are still times when this stuff comes back and stings. Not fear of the lake of fire so much, as many of the other doctrines... but that was part of it.

Further, my reflections on reincarnation give me a kind of solace that heaven and hell never did.


JPM

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: I found that as soon as I started to remember scraps of my own previous lives, the fear of death-as-extinction -- the equivalent of the damnation fear that you get if you grow up in this culture outside of a religious context -- popped like a soap bubble. I know former Christians who lost all fear of damnation the same way. One more argument for daily meditation!

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
When my mom passed away suddenly in 2008, when I was 28, and she came to visit in a series of dreams... a lot of my fear of death dissipated at that point. Something about her consciousness still being able to reach me, you could say.

Daily meditation has done wonders for anxiety I used to suffer, among other things. I feel like I have found a few possible scraps of past lives, but am not sure yet, and further details haven't surfaced.

I also feel a little bit less pressure on myself. If I don't get everything done in one life, it's not the end... thanks for that tidbit which I got from the OPW.

JPM

The fact they even use no-self is strong clue as it is a terrible translation.  Garbage in, garbage out.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
"the false notion of God as a sort of ultimate domestic abuser who demands endless cringing flattery"

Hmm. Suddenly the reason why when Western Culture went through its Rationalism the core mythic image was Man the Conqueror of Nature makes a lot more sense: if we are like God, and God is the Ultimate Domestic Abuser, then we should be the Ultimate Domestic Abuser. The whole militant atheist scene then could be seen as atheists doing their best to act out the worst of the God of the Old Testament...

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: Exactly. Whether or not we're created in the image of God in a theological sense, psychologically this is generally true: most human beings model their sense of self on the divine image or images their culture provides them. This can be a very bad thing, if the divine image is nasty enough.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
In John 3:17 it's stated that "For God did not send out His Son into the world in order that He might judge the world, but in order that the world might be saved through Him."

Paul states that when Jesus comes back riding on the clouds, 'the dead in Christ will be raised first, then we who are still alive, and we will all join Him in the air.' That is, if you die Christian you have a cosmic sticky note on you; you are the very first to get resurrected. No more judgement for you.

Paul also makes the point repeatedly, I might add, that Jesus' dying on the Cross bought you free from your sin. The first Adam sinned; Jesus as 'the second Adam' undoes the sin. Acknowledge what He did for you, and you are redeemed from Satan's pawnshop.

Revelation 20:14 "Then death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. The lake of fire is the second death."

So hell gets thrown into hell and obliterated?
Hmmm some more.

My very best wishes to you on gaining freedom from fear. I agree with the others who state that you've been spiritually abused.

-- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
As an Anglo-Catholic occultist who sometimes wrestles with the less savory aspects of the Christian
tradition, I'd also like to suggest reading some works written by Christian universalists. Even if you no longer consider yourself Christian, these critiques from within the Christian paradigm can be helpful for laying bare the intellectual and moral bankruptcy of the idea of eternal damnation, and for letting go of the idea that it's somehow "what Jesus taught."

For example, David Bentley Hart's "That All Shall Be Saved makes what I consider an irrefutable case that eternal damnation is simply incompatible with the God revealed in Christ, and that the doctrine of everlasting hell is a distortion of the Gospel that has warped the moral imaginations of the faithful for centuries. Writers like Jordan Daniel Wood (https://jordandanielwood.substack.com/p/the-future-of-hell) and David Armstrong (https://perennialdigression.substack.com/p/sunday-saunter-why-i-am-a-universalist?utm_source=publication-search) make a similar case from a specifically Catholic standpoint, and even argue that universal salvation can be reconciled with the historical teachings of the Roman Catholic Church. And, of course, it's worth looking at the sources these contemporary authors draw upon, including Christian universalists like Hans Urs von Balthazar, Sergei Bulgakov, and George MacDonald.

If you like audio media, I've also found the Grace Saves All podcast to be a valuable resource. The host is protestant, but he has had Catholic universalists on as guests, including Jordan Daniel Wood and David Bentley Hart.

I hope this helps! The works I've referenced above have been enormous sources of strength in my own spiritual journey.

Fred N.

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content:
Hi there,

This fear is incultated into Catholics generally, as you know and as JMG has said. It is done deliberately for reasons to obvious to need mentioning. I'd like to share a few things that have helped me:

1. Explore other religious traditions, including other Christian traditions. On a strict-Catholic interpretation, Hindus, Taoists, Buddhists, and even the Eastern Orthodox are subject to damnation and their beliefs, insofar as they contradict Catholic teaching, are false. Study their traditions, and try to notice how certain their teachers are of their veracity. That certainty, meanwhile, will be rooted in both philosophical speculation, spiritual experience, and revelation by divine beings. Philosophy, spiritual experience, and revelation, meanwhile, are also the only forms of evidence for the truth of Catholic teaching. How is it that one set of teachings could be definitely right while the others are entirely wrong?

We might also add miracles to our list of types of evidence, though obviously there is an overlap between all of these categories. If you study miracles long enough, you'll find that they occur in many or all religious traditions, though each has its own distinct miracle-type. Near where I live, for example, there is a Buddhist temple which sent to Vietnam for a certain number of relics. Upon their reception in America, it was discovered that six new relics had "manifested." This is a typical Buddhist miracle. In 1995, statues of Ganesha around India were observed drinking milk. This is a typical Hindu miracle. Also near my home, there is an Orthodox church which an icon of the Virgin Mary which bleeds myrrh. This is a very common Orthodox miracle. In the modern period (but going back to the Middle Ages), eucharistic miracles, in which the host bleeds or even transforms itself into human cardiac tissue, are probably the most typically Catholic miracles. Is it likelier that the world's great traditions have their own typical spiritual experiences, apparitions, revelations, and miracles, but that all are false except thsoe of the Roman Catholic Church?

2. Similar to but different from the above, study the only forms of good evidence about the after-death state that we actually have. These are, of course, the reports of near-death experiences, and children who have memories of previous incarnations. You can start with the first recorded NDE that I know if, which is in Book X of Plato's Republic (and which also confirms the reality of reincarnation.) If you keep looking, you can find databases with thousands of NDE accounts and thousands of reincarnation reports. Very few of the NDEs and none of the reincarnation reports confirm Catholic teachings about the afterlife.

Of course, none of these things can be believed with 100% certainty. But you might imagine an analogous situation, in which a foreign country was only known through travelers' reports. Imagine that there were thousands of such reports, which described the country in great detail. Then imagine that there was a powerful bureaucracy which had written its own description of the foreign country, based almost entirely on ideas developed by academics and not on exploration, which matched none of the travelers reports. Indeed, this bureaucracy prohibited actual exploration of the foreign country, and threatened harsh penalties against anyone who dared to explore it or even to believe the accounts of those who had.

Which are more likely to be true-- the travelers' reports, or the claims of the bureaucrats?

(For that matter, you might consider how closely my imaginary bureaucracy parallels the behavior of actual bureaucracies, such as those that govern the American healthcare system.)

3. This is a bit dark, but you might find it helpful: Read the grand jury reports that came out a decade ago on the Catholic Church in Pennsylvania, and similar reports of abuse by clergy around the US and around the world. Keep in mind that a lot of the psychological abuse that the Church engages in is really psycho-sexual abuse: Children are taught, at the moment they begin awakening to their normal, human sexuality, that any sexual activity or even any sexual *thought* outside of "PIV" sex with their husband or wife is a mortal sin subject to damnation. Given the way human beings actually work, this is akin to telling them that eating or even imagining eating food is a mortal sin: Tell someone like that, and they will eventually get hungry, start daydreaming about a sandwich, and eventually go and eat something. It would be bad enough if this were the extent of the abuse: But note that the same hierarchy which teaches 14 year old boys that they are damned if they imagine having sex has also permitted their priests to engage in horrific acts of actual sexual abuse. So a priest who rapes an altar boy in a hearse on the way to a funeral is protected, but you're damned if you daydream about your girlfriend. Can an organization that behaves this way really be trusted?

4. Finally, if you still find that you want to participate in Catholic religion at all, it's worth spending some time exploring what that religion has actually historically looked like. One interesting place to start might be a book written about a century ago, entitled Christmas in Ritual and Tradition. It can be read online here: https://www.sacred-texts.com/time/crt/index.htm . I recommend this volume because it gives detailed accounts of what the peoples' actual celebration of Christmas and the many holidays from Halloween to Epiphany actually looked like. You can then ask yourself the questions: Does the Catholic religion consist of the rules and opinions of the church bureaucracy, or might it really consist of the actual beliefs and behaviors of the people? When these two are in conflict (as they very frequently are), is there any reason at all to prefer the rules and opinions of the bureaucracy?

replies.title: Re: Fear
replies.content: One thing that worked for me when I had those thoughts (although I was raised Baptist, not Catholic) was to think about whether I really believed that *other* people would be eternally damned for not being Christian. For some reason, it always seemed obviously false that others who didn't profess the right theological beliefs or belong to a particular religion while alive would be eternally tortured for it after death, even when I worried that I might be. Ironically enough, I never could quite accept the idea of eternal damnation as a consequence of mistaken beliefs when I was a Christian, but I also had a heck of a time shaking it once I wasn't one any more!

comments.title: Shining and Occult Mead and Question on Evola
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May the cold weather that we're finally getting here in Texas do some good for those who get it.

To Share: Eirik Westcoat, an American esotericist and poet who is a master in the Rune Gild wrote a pair of very interesting books, meant to go together: Occult Mead, a series of essays on esoteric topics, and Shining Mead a collection of poetry in modern English following medieval Germanic alliterative meter on a wide range of topics (mostly esoteric, but not only). While there is a fairly strong emphasis on the Runes, as you might expect, neither book is solely of interest to Heathen-leaning folks, and a central theme of both that might be of interest to those here (and has bearing on my question below) is the Grail quest, and the argument that the stone Moldavite is a good candidate as a material for Grail-focused tools. (Oh, and a quick aside: the cover of Occult Mead features a modified version of the symbol usually known as the "Black Sun," but this is not an endorsement of the uses to which its been put, but rather because the final essay in the volume argues that the design in Wewelsberg Castle was meant to represent both a sun wheel and the Round Table, and Westcoat thinks its worth reclaiming as such. Myself, despite finding his case credible, I'm not so sure it isn't past the point of no-return, especially given its origin).

To Ask: As mentioned above, Westcoat spends a good deal of OM talking about the importance of the Grail myth to his spiritual path, and given its links to your material, I've considered whether it might become a part of mine. Westcoat gives some hints on how to comfortably mesh that with Heathenry and Runelore, though he takes a more Thorssonian approach as an "Odian" (emulate Odin rather than necessarily worship Him), which I'm not entirely on board with. Westcoat speaks approvingly of Evola's The Mystery of the Grail, and I know that you have not been overly impressed with Evola's work overall, saying, if I remember right, that most of what's good in his works can be found better stated by those he was influenced by. All that's just context for my actual question: given all that, do you have any thoughts on The Mystery of the Grail and how worthwhile it is for incorporating the Grail myth into spiritual practice?

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Shining and Occult Mead and Question on Evola
replies.content:
1) The design in Wewelsburg was indeed meant to represent both those things; Heinrich Himmler was a very knowledgeable occultist, though an evil and misguided one. He wanted his own Round Table of SS "knights" in his fallen and degraded faux-Camelot.

2) Evola is always worth reading and never safe to rely on. I'd encourage you to read his Grail book, but only in the process of studying Grail literature more generally. I find it amusing to note that of all the books out there on the Grail, the one closest to Evola's is Arthur Edward Waite's The Holy Grail: Tha Galahad Quest in the Arthurian Literature. Both are scholarly, thoughtful books by deeply biased authors who skew the whole body of lore to fit their personal obsessions; the fact that their obsessions are so utterly opposed to one another just puts icing on the cake.

Any work done with the Grail tradition, at least in my opinion, should be based on original sources, the earlier the better. All the scholarship out there (and yes, I include my Grail book in this!) is no more than hand-me-downs. Start with Chretien de Troyes and Wolfram von Eschenbach and you'll be much less likely to go wrong.

replies.content: Ahem.  If you read the rest of the paragraph that follows that sentence you'll find my explanation.

comments.title: Archangel Michael
comments.content:
It has recently come into my possession a relic of sorts and I feel compelled to ask about it here seeing as how now I am a bit wary of it. For context, I now have in my collection a white stone housed in a delicate reliquary from The Sanctuary of Monte Sant'Angelo on Mount Gargano, Italy. I gave a reliquary to my sister as well, since I had two. When I meditate now I wear that around my neck , and I began to pray to St. Michael , as I had noted in a prior post after coming to a series of realizations and even what appeared to be the beginning of an out of body experience.

I am a resident of the United States. My family is of Italian origin; many reside in southern Italy where this jewlery originated. It has a deep religious and cultural import to me: legends of Archangel Michael are intertwined with much of that landscape and also featured prominantly in the myths of my Catholic upbringing although I would say I hardly identify as that anymore. The occult and Idealist teachings have offered me a thesis of the world that does not force me to exclude circumstantial evidence. I have yet to find a working theory of the world yet as explanatory as that od the Idealisr/occultist. If there is one: do shown me!

The reason I am expositing all of this is - although I do not necessarily expect to connect with a being as advanced as an archangel - I did not expect to read about how dangerous it might be. On a prior magic Monday, our host had even expounded how a meeting with this archangel was like a encountering a bolt of lightning. I cannot imagine what meeting such a raw force of nature would be like; but, I do not think I am spiritually advanced to do so as of yet. My question is: in addition to praying to other beings, is it beneficial or even safe to continue to ask St. Michael for protection/guidance as I have been or ought I redirect my energies to a "lower" angel: one much closer to my reach? I also pray and thank my guardian angel, who I feel a much more immediate/loving presence with.

replies.title: Re: Archangel Michael
replies.content:
Nah, don't worry about it. Michael is tremendously powerful, but he has been invoked for protection for thousands of years with good results.

My experience was a very intense one, but I was never in any danger. It was clear to me that if the archangel chose to strike me dead, you'd have trouble even finding the ashes, but it was just as clear to me that the overwhelming power he wielded was under perfect control, and guided by perfect compassion. I hope you have the same experience someday -- it's a real education.

The archangels, by the way, are entirely capable of modulating their force so that they don't harm humans. Have you ever read the apocryphal Book of Tobit? The archangel Raphael is an important character; he takes on human disguise, as angels generally do in the Old Testament, and accompanies the protagonist Tobias on his quest. It's a good lively story and has much to teach.

replies.content:

Thank you. It truky allays my fear to know that you are speaking from experience. When I askes my guardian angel if it was "safe", I got a very similar response. That being said, I am still so new at this all, I do not yet have tbe ability to parse out the wheat from the chaff.

replies.title: Re: Archangel Michael
replies.content:
The (arch)angels Michael and Gabriel are both mentioned in the current Hebrew Tanakh (Bible), which does not mention Raphael or Uriel.

Raphael figures in the book of Tobit, which is part of both the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Bibles, but not Protestant Bibles (where it is found among the apocrypha).

Uriel figures in the book which is called "Second Esdras" among the apocrypha of the King James Bible and "Fourth Esdras" in Medieval Roman Catholic Bibles. The Roman Catholic Church expelled it (and "Third Esdras") from its (Latin Vulgate) Bible by a decree of the 16th-century Council of Trent, and since then has excluded it from its printed Bibles.

Among the Eastern Orthodox Churches, only the Russian and other Slavic ones include it in their printed Bibles, where they call it "Third Esdras." The Greek Orthodox Church does not include it in its printed Bibles.

These four books of Esdras can be very confusing. In the King James Bible "First Esdras" is called Ezra, and "Second Esdras" is called Nehemiah. The other two are among the apocryphal books of the King James Bible, where they are called "First Esdras" and "Second Esdras" (NB "Esdras," not "Ezra").

Despite being very intense, I never felt in any danger from the energy. Angelic energies displace evil and earthly murkiness very effectively and I'm fine with that, but they also feel very far above me, very ethereal. Your perception will be coloured by your own capabilities and experience, though.

comments.title: A Thorny Divination
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

1) A friend appears to have suddenly blocked me on all messenger apps. I say "appears to" because the whole thing is rather mystifying to me—there was, up to that moment, no indication whatsoever that I had done anything to offend him. This is the second time something like this has happened, though, and I'm the only common factor between those situations.

Given that I can no longer ask him what's up, I turned to divination instead and, as this is obviously a rather emotionally charged issue, I'd appreciate your help interpreting the following SGO spread:

Me: Extension
Friend: Necessity
My inability to message him: Dispersal
The reason: Expansion
The reason I can no longer message him: Isolation
The reason for the Dispersal: Impossibility
Is it me or him?: Harmony
Is this just a temporary situation?: Excess

So, here's my take: I don't really understand what Extension is supposed to signify. Necessity seems to indicate that this situation had to happen, possibly on his end—my tentative assumption is that it's been brewing for a while, though unbeknownst to me, and he simply could no longer stand it.

The next four factors are poorly formulated—I really wanted to know why he's blocked me but wasn't sure if that was what's really going on, so I left the question a bit too open. I originally interpreted Dispersal to mean that the friendship had simply dispersed but now it's occurring to me that this might also be referring to the messages being thrown out by the chat systems. Then I drew Expansion for clarification which, if it refers to the situation as a whole, might mean that this started out as a small annoyance that blew up—uncomfortably reminiscent of the first such situation—but there's another take on this I'll get to later. Isolation presumably refers to the state of being blocked. Impossibility depends on how I interpret Dispersal: if it's about the friendship, that would indicate that it could go on no longer, while if it is about the messages, they're being dispersed because it's impossible for them to be delivered.

The other option for Expansion is that it somehow relates to Extension, given that it's a somewhat similar motion. Last time, my life ultimately improved, if only after a depressive spiral that nearly ended in front of a train. Thinking about the symbolism of a line connecting two points, I do feel as though I'm in between two points in my life, no longer really holding onto the first but also not really moving towards the second—I'm not seeing the extension to infinity here but it is curiously similar to the state I was in during the first time this situation occurred. I can see, though, why that friendship had to end, whereas I struggle to see any upside this time around.

"Is it me or him" was a bit of a desperate attempt at deflecting blame which appears to be answered with "actually, the two of you made this mess together." Excess, I'd interpret as this situation exceeding being temporary, i.e. being permanent.

2) Not a question, just something I wanted to share:

I seem to recall you saying something about Marian spirituality and Christianity moving towards immanence as opposed to transcendence. Related to this, when I took up a daily prayer to Odin, I experienced a brief spout of fascination with High Church Christianity, particularly Catholicism, that I can only sum up as "are you sure you want to leave?" (2.5: could that be an effect of being baptised?). However, when I actually started think about Christian (and particularly Catholic) teaching, I found that (beyond the pretty straightforward moral stuff) it simply doesn't do anything for me anymore—there's no resonance there.

However, last week, I suddenly remembered a song, Maria durch ein Dornwald ging, that I found oddly moving. A translation:

Maria walks amid the thorns,
Kyrie eleison,
Maria walks amid the thorns,
Which seven years no leaf has born.
Jesus and Maria.

What 'neath her heart doth Mary bear?
Kyrie eleison,
The little Child doth Mary bear,
Beneath her heart He nestles there.
Jesus and Maria.

Lo! roses on the thorns appear!
Kyrie eleison,
As the two are passing near,
Lo! roses on the thorns appear!
Jesus and Maria.

—https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maria_durch_ein_Dornwald_ging


I had been under the impression that this was a widely attested miracle, given the association of Mary with roses, but the only source I found is this one German song. Wikipedia says that it first appears in print in 1850, though some form of it likely dates back to at least 1608.

—David P.

comments.title: Magical fasting and the salt jar
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thank you for making this forum available and taking the time to answer our questions. I have a few today:

1) I've become interested in the concept of directing a period of fasting toward a particular intention. Do you think this could be effective even if I was already planning to fast for health reasons, or does it need to be a fast undertaken for the sole purpose of magical action? (I'm experimenting with 5:2 intermittent fasting and wandering if I can put the fast days to magical use.)

2) If so, how would one go about it? I'm thinking of simply saying a short prayer at the beginning of the day dedicating the fast toward my intention, and then turning my mind toward the desired end whenever hunger catches my attention throughout the day. Would this work, and would you recommend any additional actions?

3) Is it important that the container used in the "salt jar" protective charm be made of glass? Or would anything that doesn't block etheric energies (i.e., not silk or lead) work?

4) In his Practical Guide to Qabalistic Symbolism, Gareth Knight talks about the need for a closing ritual after all workings of "practical occultism", by which he seems to mean things like pathworking as well as enchantment. Do you agree that this is always necessary, and if so, how elaborate does the closing need to be? Would something as simple as tracing a final sign of the Cross (for a Christian occultist) with the intention of closing the working suffice?

Thanks again and have an excellent week,

Fred N.

replies.title: Re: Magical fasting and the salt jar
replies.content:
1) It needs to be done solely for the magical purpose. Yes, you can have health in the back of your mind, but dedicate each fast with prayer and attention toward a specific goal. That's powerful magic, btw.

2) That would do very well.

3) Anything crystalline, such as pottery, will diffuse some of the effect. Wood is a natural transmitter of etheric energy, so a wooden container is good, but glass is best -- it's actually a liquid, if a very thick one, and so ether passes through it easily.

replies.title: Re: Magical fasting and the salt jar
replies.content: Thanks! Regarding question 1, if I had started a practice of fasting twice a week with the initial thought that it might improve my metabolic health, do I need to do anything special to "repurpose" this practice for a different aim? Or is it enough to dedicate the fast to the magical intent as described in question 2, and try to redirect my thoughts to the new goal whenever I catch myself thinking of it in health-related terms?

replies.title: Re: Magical fasting and the salt jar
replies.content: Just dedicate each individual fast to the purpose you have in mind, and refocus your thoughts on the purpose whenever you feel hungry.

replies.title: Re: Magical fasting and the salt jar
replies.content:
Thank you as always for your very clear advice.

- Fred N.

replies.title: Re: Magical fasting and the salt jar
replies.content: Not our esteemed host, but since it looks like the question hasn't been answered yet, yes, some sort of closing is important. It should include an expression of gratitude to any forces you invited to your work along with a polite but firm "invitation" for them to return to whatever sphere or plane you invited them from, and some sort of ritual gesture that "closes" the work, meaning you close the door to the other worlds you have opened and put yourself firmly back in the midst of the material reality. This last could be striking a chime or ringing a bell, clapping your hands once, or stomping your foot. I think those are the key points, but if I missed anything, please community, fill in any gaps.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Hello commenters,
Wishing everyone a great start to the week!

A few random questions, if you don’t mind:

1. I read about personal conflicts and wars here on MM last week, and also on some subreddit on Reddit. I think it was in the Spiritualism sub, and it was a mediumistic transcription. It seems that, here on the material plane, conflicts are allowed, but not on the mental plane. There seems to be a natural law (like gravity) that prevents conflicts there. The big question is: why would personal and national conflicts be allowed on this plane?

2. Do you know which authors directly influenced Blavatsky?

3. I admit I’m quite a newbie when it comes to Eastern spirituality, but I received an academic text on Vedanta. Do you know of any author who adapted Vedantic theories and practices to a Western and initiatic context?

4. And I bought a used book on occultism at a secondhand bookstore last week. Any tips on how to clear it of the influences of its former owner and of everyone who handled it?

Thank you for the help and for your promptness here on Magic Monday!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On #2:

Blavatsky was very highly educated as child and young woman, and she was extremely widely read in perhaps as many as a half-dozen languages: her native Russian, of course, and also French, English, German, possibly Latin, Italian, and a few others (Georgian, Arabic, etc.). According to some accounts, it seems likely that she also had an eidetic memory, which would mean that she could re-read or quote in the privacy of her own head any book of interest that she had once read in material form.

Until recently no one had really looked at all deeply into her non-English sources, or even very deeply into her English ones.

This has begun to change now, thanks to a very fine Swiss scholar named Urs App. Just this year he published The Mother of All Religions: The Genesis of Blavatsky's Theosophy: Ancient Theology, Orientalism, and Buddhism (2025). Yet there is still a very great deal more to do beyond the substantial amount that App has already accomplished.

Among other sources yet to be explored are a very large number of relevant works available to Blavatsky in Russian, especially during her childhood in Tiflis: App does not include even one book i Russian among the sources he has looked into. Blavatsky's wider family included a considerable number of men and women with esoteric interests and the private libraries to support their interests. For one minor example, it was Blavatsky's own mother who translated and published a number of Bulwer-Lytton's occult novels into Russian.

replies.title: HPB
replies.content:
I also recommend it.

As for Russian sources, and what might have been available to her: she seems to have been very influential in the world of Russian esotericism. And since those authors were likely to be familiar with the Russian literature on which she drew, at least they did not seem to regard her as a plagiarist, for example, as some contemporary western critics did.

In addition, what we now call esotericism does not seem to have been so alien to ordinary Russian thought. There's quite a bit of Russian th4ology and philosophy that would seem esoteric to western European readers, only some of which has been translated, though more has been coming out recently.

LeGrand

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
More on Blavatsky's reading material as a young woman in Russia:

Although no one has (as yet) looked into the esoteric literature available to her in Russia, one can get some preliminary idea of what was available from a rare private library catalogue published in only 75 copies, listing the esoteric contents of Count Uvarov's own library: A Ladrague, Sciences secrètes (Bibliothèque Ouvaroff: Catalogue spécemen)) (Moscow, 1870). There is a PDF available on google books.

There is also a more general bibliography in Russian for Russia, by Ivan Kazimirovich Antoshevskii, Bibliografiia okkul'tizma [Bibliography of Occultism] (1911, 35 pp.), which I have never been able to find either as a hard copy or as a PDF online.

comments.title: Regarding Magical Syncretism
comments.content:
Hi JGM,

  Thank you for hosting another Magic Monday.
 
On the last occasion you wrote in response to someone asking about combining practices from different traditions: “Every serious magical authority disapproves of cherrypicking. Every one of them also does it.”

  I have been thinking about this in practical terms. Currently I am working through the GSF material. However,  lately I started to venture into Hellenic polytheism motivated by your encouragement to establish a religious devotion alongside occult and magical studies and practice.

So, I got some material to understand the whole outlook better and to get into the practical side of it.   But, of course, you know how it goes: You start with Hellenic polytheism, slip into Neoplatonism and sooner or later you arrive at classical theurgy. ;) Then you come across some working or ritual that seems relevant and interesting. And with it the question arises: “Do I integrate it into my practice - or is that something I would do sometime later?”

 My question is this:   When starting such “syncretisms” for personal work what are sensible guidelines or methods of discernment?   

My approach would be to analyze the aim, structure and functioning of any particular practice and then see whether it blends and most importantly that it does not interfere with anything else I am currently doing.

  I would also only take more open and isolated practices into consideration - not workings from wholly established traditions that constitute a complete system. What I mean by this: I would not take something like the Setting of the Wards of Power from the Ogdoadic tradition and then perform the SoP afterwards. But I do have some literature on Hellenic practice which includes protection rituals for e.g. the household involving, say, Zeus Herkeios. That would be something I would consider compatible.  

(Incidentally exactly the topic of cleansing and protection reminded me of your cleansing bath which itself might be a good example for how to blend different traditions together.  )

Then I would check for the basic category of the working. As in the above example: SoP is for balancing and protection - not for cleansing as such. So, a cleansing practice would fit in.

Passing these “filters” I would simply try it out and see whether it works as intended without throwing something else off. And if it doesn’t after a few weeks I would conclude that all is well.   

Any thoughts or recommendations on this general question and the outlined approach?

Thank you very much!  

- Florian

replies.title: Re: Regarding Magical Syncretism
replies.content:
Every experienced occultist I know works out his or her own system for integrating new material into existing practices. My approach is to do divination first, to make sure it's a good idea; then I start doing it separately, a few hours from anything else, until I'm sure I understand it and have a good sense of how the energy works with my existing practices; then I adjust as needed.

The practices you've outlined, however, should fit the GSF material very easily, and your approach seems sensible enough.

replies.title: Re: Regarding Magical Syncretism
replies.content:

I am in no rush and will let things unfold slowly and methodically.

replies.content:

2) I haven't seen anything in print on the subject -- I've simply noted my own experiences and those of others who have discussed it.

replies.title: re: insomnia
replies.content:
I have this problem now and then.

Some things that've helped:

-A really dense book that I only read at bedtime, but consistently, a little every night, after I climb into the blankets: read till my eyes go buggy, or I stop comprehending anything *then* click the lamp off. Economics, theology, and philosophy all very efficacious. Complete works of St. Basil helped me sleep for months.

-A protocol for what I am going to *do instead* if I absolutely cannot sleep (NOT scrolling the internet): in my case, prayer book, psalms, prayer rope. Because sometimes sleep just isn't going to happen. If I have a backup plan, I don't feel like the time was wasted, I don't lie awake in bed for hours miserable, and (bonus points) if I can get into a really solid meditative state, it's almost as good as having slept. I don't feel as tired in the morning as I would if I'd simply thrashed about in bed, being anxious about not sleeping. Sometimes you're not insomniac, you're just being given an opportunity for intense prayer and meditation ;)

-no coffee after 11am, and sometimes I just need to cut way back or leave off entirely.

-it's been mentioned in other MMs, but if the anxiety is really extreme: consider that it might not be you, and take counter-measures appropriate to *an attack*. I read Psalm 90. YMMV, but this works so well and so quick for anxiety attacks, that there is no longer any doubt in my mind that these are external phenomena, not just "me being irrationally anxious". Sometimes it's not *you* that needs addressing: it's something gnawing on you, and you gotta fight back, St. George style.

-Salt. I don't know why, and this is a personal observation not advice, but sometimes I just need like a quarter teaspoon of salt in a small glass of water.

-Specifically on the having to get up in the night to urinate... I understand that happens to everyone with age, to some extent. But personally: it seems related to insulin/glucose levels as well, and if I keep a tight lid on how late I eat anything-- like no food or caloric beverages after 5pm-- suddenly I can sleep all night, no pit stops. Again, personal observation, not medical advice.

Anyway, been there, done that, and I hope you find something that works for you.

Over the years, I've encountered places with a very strong "feeling" about them, like a presence. I know of one small, picturesque city that many people love, but which to me feels heavy and sad. I know another place that I can only describe as feeling "out of time", like it's still holding the energy of 100 years ago. Some place feel good an wholesome. Others feel "off". And it has nothing to do with material circumstances.

My question for JMG would be, is this sort of affinity for a place a thing that happens often, and if so why? Is it an egregor thing, or something to do with land spirits, or some other energies or beings? Just curious if you have any insight into this. I also wonder if I'm somewhat more sensitive to it?

comments.content:
Hello John,

I have been thinking about morality a lot, how much of religion and even mysticism emphasises good moral practise. and yet, there is are certain realities, in particular around capitalism and the necessity to participate in the world, to compete, in order to earn a living. In this environment i feel the need to be cunning sometimes, or find myself being manipulative. I feel a sense of guilt when i think about this, for example the need to be assertive, and the instinct for self preservation, and need for expansion and conquering, as a whole. I try my best not to hurt people, recognise that as a spiritual law of sorts, and yet at the same time there is some "hurt" caused simply by living and participating in this world.

Im not sure if i've worded this properly, but i hope you get the gist. My question i suppose, is how does one find the balance, between self preservation vs living in a way that's in harmony with the cosmos ?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Morality is much more complex than it's made to look by some spiritual traditions. You might find it useful to read Alasdair MacIntyre's book After Virtue, which explores the classical tradition of virtue ethics. Cunning is not necessarily wrong, and self-assertion is only a vice when it's taken to grotesque extremes -- how can you love your neighbor as yourself when you don't love yourself?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for the reply. I will look into the book you mentioned. How would you suggest one gets over these feelings of guilt, and orienting themselves to a new sense of morality that works better, while still obeying spiritual law? Is it just a case of trial and error?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I recommend working with the exercises of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which will help you resolve the childhood conflicts and traumas that usually lie beneath the difficulties you've described. You can access them here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Aristotle and Siddhartha converged upon the same truth: it's all about the Middle Path! Vices are in extreme, virtue is in moderation. Or as the Archdruid says, the opposite of one bad idea is usually another bad idea.

comments.title: Protection from nasty vibes
comments.content:
In the town to which I moved just over a year ago I've encountered some very unpleasant homophobic vibes, thus far for the most part just nasty verbal altercations.

People who haven't experienced anything of the sort may not be aware that such incidents are not just words you can brush off with unconcern; what begins as a verbal taunt can and sometimes does escalate into violent assault. It's like being called the n-word when you're black. I don't feel altogether safe. And even if nothing of the sort happens, it taints your day. You start wondering if each person you pass might not be such a mean bigot, ready with a vicious word.

At your suggestion a while back I invoked the protection of Saturn to deal with a particular stalker, and that seems to have worked. But this morning I woke with a premonition that something of the sort would happen and sure enough it did: not the same person but much the same sort of thing.

If a handy Algol election were coming up I would make a talisman; but elections are few and far between. Can you recommend anything in the meantime that I might do to ward off such nastiness? I've had a very difficult three years and I don't need any additional poop from poop-heads.

I'm familiar with basic Hellenic Golden Dawn magic, know the fundamentals of traditional astrology, and have a copy of your Encyclopedia of Natural Magic. (I have others of your books too, but those are far away in storage.)

replies.title: Re: Protection from nasty vibes
replies.content: Do you practice a daily banishing ritual? That would be my starting point. You might also try the red bag amulet discussed in the Magic Monday FAQ.
